items.front: What does a through clearance authorize?
items.back: It authorizes clearing an aircraft through intermediate stops.

items.front: What is the basic phraseology for a through clearance?
items.back: Cleared through (airport) to (fix).

items.front: In through-clearance phraseology, what follows the word through?
items.back: The intermediate-stop airport.

items.front: In through-clearance phraseology, what follows the word to?
items.back: The final fix.

items.front: What phrase is used when an aircraft will operate in an approved ALTRV?
items.back: Via approved altitude reservation (mission name) flight plan.

items.front: When is the ALTRV phrase inserted into the clearance?
items.back: When the aircraft will operate in an approved ALTRV.

items.front: What phases does normal ALTRV coverage usually include?
items.back: Departure, climb, cruise, and arrival.

items.front: Normal ALTRV coverage usually extends to what point?
items.back: To the holding pattern or the point or time ATC provides separation.

items.front: Does normal ALTRV coverage end automatically at top of climb?
items.back: No.

items.front: What words surround the mission name in the ALTRV phrase?
items.back: Altitude reservation before it, and flight plan after it.

items.front: If a flight will operate IFR first and then VFR, where is it cleared?
items.back: To the fix where the IFR part of the flight ends.

items.front: How is a VFR-then-IFR flight treated at departure?
items.back: As a VFR departure.

items.front: When is the IFR clearance issued to a VFR-then-IFR flight?
items.back: When the aircraft requests it approaching the fix where IFR begins.

items.front: What code should be assigned to a Mode C aircraft changing from VFR to IFR?
items.back: A beacon code that will allow MSAW alarms.

items.front: What must ATC ask a below-minimum-altitude pilot requesting IFR?
items.back: Whether the pilot can maintain terrain and obstruction clearance until reaching the appropriate minimum IFR altitude.

items.front: If the pilot can maintain terrain and obstruction clearance until reaching the proper minimum altitude, what may ATC issue?
items.back: The IFR clearance.

items.front: If the pilot cannot maintain terrain and obstruction clearance until reaching the proper minimum altitude, what should ATC instruct?
items.back: Maintain VFR and state intentions.

items.front: Who is responsible for terrain and obstruction clearance until the appropriate minimum altitude is reached in the pop-up IFR case?
items.back: The pilot.

items.front: What must ATC not assign or imply below the MIA or MEA in this pop-up IFR case?
items.back: Specific course guidance.

items.front: What must be verified first before processing an airfile request unless other coordination exists?
items.back: That the aircraft is within your area of jurisdiction.

items.front: After jurisdiction is confirmed, what information should be obtained from an airfile aircraft?
items.back: The information needed to provide IFR service.

items.front: What are the three basic airfile outcomes listed in 4-2-9?
items.back: Issue a destination clearance, issue a short-range clearance, or instruct the aircraft to contact flight service if the flight plan cannot be processed.

items.front: What phrase is used if the airfile aircraft is cleared to the destination airport?
items.back: Cleared to (destination) airport.

items.front: If a clearance is to a NAVAID and the type is known, what must follow the name?
items.back: The type of NAVAID.

items.front: If a clearance is to an intersection or waypoint and the type is known, what must follow the name?
items.back: The type of intersection or waypoint.

items.front: Do the airfile procedures imply that airfile processing has priority over another ATC duty?
items.back: No.

items.front: Before switching an arriving IFR aircraft to CTAF at an airport without a tower or FSS, what must be provided if needed?
items.back: Instructions on how to cancel the IFR flight plan.

items.front: What is the cancellation instruction when the airport has an air/ground communications station?
items.back: Report cancellation of IFR on (frequency).

items.front: What is one approved cancellation instruction when the airport has no air/ground communications station?
items.back: Report cancellation of IFR this frequency or with flight service.

items.front: What is the other approved no-station cancellation instruction form?
items.back: Report cancellation of IFR this frequency or with the serving FSS or ATC controlling facility.

items.front: How should ATC acknowledge receipt of the IFR cancellation?
items.back: IFR cancellation received.

items.front: In the no-station cancellation phrase, what word separates this frequency from the alternate reporting outlet?
items.back: Or.

items.front: At a no-station airport, must the pilot report cancellation only to flight service?
items.back: No. The pilot may be instructed to report it this frequency or with flight service or the named facility.

items.front: What should happen after a pilot reports IFR cancellation on frequency?
items.back: ATC should acknowledge the report.
